serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Mysore Fry is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a moderate restaurant or home-cooked portion of spiced fried mutton pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Mysore Fry — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Mutton Mysore Fry (cr_id G00010881) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this entry. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Spice Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate spiced meat preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Mysore Fry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous spiced meat dishes with comparable carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g), predominantly from onion, tomato, and spice paste. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.9; rounded to 2.6 per 100 g equivalent. GL per serving (150 g): (35 × 11.16) / 100 ≈ 3.9. The low GI reflects the protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate profile of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal, request reduced salt in preparation, avoid pairing with other high-sodium condiments or sauces, and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake against the recommended ceiling of 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) support postprandial glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Mysore Fry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–150 g) due to its low carbohydrate load and high protein content; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (11.58 g/100g) and elevated sodium (467 mg/100g) combined with cholesterol (85 mg/100g) require portion control for cardiovascular patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Mysore Fry to occasional consumption (once per week or less), keep portions to 100 g, choose lean mutton cuts, minimise added oil during frying, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate the impact of saturated fat and sodium on lipid profiles and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid) combined with dry-fry preparation generating advanced glycation end products (AGEs) amplifies joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Mysore Fry; if consumed, prefer braised or stewed mutton preparations over frying to reduce AGE formation, and pair with anti-inflammatory foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Mysore Fry is a South Indian dry-fried mutton dish originating from the Mysore region of Karnataka, India, and widely consumed in Singapore's Indian community. The dish is prepared by marinating mutton pieces in a blend of turmeric, red chilli, coriander, cumin, ginger, and garlic, then dry-frying with onions, tomatoes, and curry leaves until the spice coating is caramelised and the meat is tender with a slightly crisp exterior. The result is a deeply aromatic, intensely spiced dish with a dry, bold texture and rich umami flavour from the reduced spice paste. Per 100 g, it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, 7.44 g carbohydrate, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high sodium content makes it unsuitable for hypertension patients without portion control. The low carbohydrate load and high protein content make it broadly compatible with Type 2 Diabetes management. The moderate fat and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) require moderation for cardiovascular patients. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a side dish at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, paired with rice, roti, or paratha at lunch and dinner. The dish is halal and gluten-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Mysore Fry (cr_id G00010881) with a provenance confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB Foodo entry for Mutton Mysore Fry does not report saturated fat breakdown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB source for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. All core macros (protein_g 14.05, fat_g 11.58, carbohydrate_g 7.44) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Mysore Fry was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 150 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.9 g. Halal status assumed based on mutton preparation without pork or alcohol; kosher status not confirmed. Dietary flag is_gluten_free set to true as no wheat-based ingredients are standard in this preparation; individual restaurant preparations may vary.
